I'm verifying that all the acceptance criteria are met end-to-end: the eval scheme is properly registered as a closed enum, all six dimensions are queryable without additional code, and the tree displays the complete chain to each primitive's evaluation with artifact references captured and visible in the detail view.

For acceptance #4, I've confirmed that when newer model version evidence arrives, the verdict gets superseded and dependent guidance is flagged as stale through the existing cascade mechanism, while immutable observations remain active and both model versions stay independently queryable.

Acceptance #5 (the eval-writeup compiler) is the one criterion I haven't demonstrated since it requires new code - a recursive renderer - which I'm flagging but not building now.

Now I need to address the core question about whether this demands refactoring of the cb beliefs framework. The worked example itself requires zero changes to the belief graph - it's self-contained and validates on its own. For real integration, there's exactly one additive change needed: adding the eval scheme to the artifact-scheme enum through the framework's normal immutable-evolution path (superseding the existing contract), which has zero blast radius since nothing depends on it and the verifier finds the active enum by role, not by id. Everything else - the new verifier check, the optional filters, the eval-writeup renderer, and the eval collections themselves - lives outside the belief graph in library code. There's no structural refactoring anywhere: no schema redesign, no new record types, no field migrations, and the belief structure itself remains untouched.

content.text:
Done - the worked example is built as a self-contained `sdl:` collection and validated end-to-end against the real tooling. Here is the deliverable and the answer to your question.

## The JSON (under the recommended placement)

Files: `examples/eval-provenance/{manifest.json,beliefs.json}` (nothing under `beliefs/` was touched). The representative primitive - note where each of the spec's 9 provenance fields lands:

```json
{
  "id": "sdl:a1",
  "type": "primitive",
  "kind": "observation",
  "domain": "eval",
  "tags": ["eval-evidence", "outcome:silent_loss"],          // outcome  -> tag (queryable via tag:)
  "claim": "On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) omitted record #7 from a 12-record bulk write and emitted no warning; the deterministic field-diff ruler scored the outcome silent_loss.",
  "artifact": "eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/case7/deterministic-fielddiff",  // eval_id/run_id/case_id/ruler -> identity URI
  "evidence": [
    { "date": "2026-06-05",
      "detail": "Harness: inspect. Deterministic field-diff ...; record #7 absent, no warning. 1 of 12 records lost.",  // harness + value
      "artifact": "document:logs/run3/case7.json" }            // artifact_ref (raw log) -> evidence.artifact
  ],
  "subjects": [                                                 // the queryable categorical dims
    { "ref": "eval/silent-data-loss-v1", "type": "eval" },
    { "ref": "run/run3", "type": "run" },
    { "ref": "case/case7", "type": "case" },
    { "ref": "model/claude-opus-4-8", "type": "model" },
    { "ref": "model-version/claude-opus-4-8@2026-01", "type": "model_version" },
    { "ref": "ruler/deterministic-fielddiff", "type": "ruler" }
  ],
  "deps": [],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-06-05"
}
```

The full collection is the spec's worked example, atomic per `(run, case, ruler)`: `sdl:a1` (deterministic) + `sdl:a2` (llm-judge) = the two ruler observations; `sdl:a3` = the cross-ruler agreement compound `deps:[a1,a2]`; `sdl:a4` = the model-version-scoped verdict `deps:[a3]`; `sdl:a5` = downstream guidance `deps:[a4]`; plus `sdl:c1`, the artifact-scheme enum that makes `eval` a closed, registered scheme.

## Validated end-to-end (zero framework changes)

| # | Acceptance criterion | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | create eval primitive; invalid/missing rejected | **PASS** - `cb.verify.schema` 13 passed / 0 failed; `eval` discovered by role and closed-enum-checked |
| 2 | query by eval_id, model(+version), case, ruler, outcome | **PASS** - every dimension queryable today: slash-ref subjects (`bs list model/claude-opus-4-8`) for values, `subject_type:ruler` for dimensions, `tag:outcome:silent_loss` for outcome |
| 3 | `bs tree` shows chain down to artifact_ref | **PASS to the `eval:` URI** - tree renders verdict→compound→primitives, each primitive's `eval:` grounding URI shown; raw-log `artifact_ref` is captured and shown by `bs show` (detail), and needs a ~1-line `tree_lines` tweak to also appear in the tree |
| 4 | newer model_version flags dependents stale | **PASS via the corrected design** - superseding the *verdict* (not the immutable observations) flags `sdl:a5` stale through the existing `--cascade`; both model_versions independently queryable |
| 5 | compile root implication → markdown writeup | **not built** - this is the one criterion that genuinely needs new code (a recursive renderer; the existing compiler is a flat `id→.claim` dereferencer) |

The granularity rule, immutability, cross-ruler agreement, and the model-version pivot all hold against the live tooling - no `confidence` score, no new record type, no struct change.

## Your question: does this demand any refactoring of cb beliefs?

**Essentially no - and nothing structural.**

- **The worked example: zero changes to the cb graph.** It is a self-contained `sdl:` collection that verifies standalone (proven above). Building and running eval collections never touches `beliefs/beliefs.json`.

- **The real integration: exactly one *additive* change to the cb graph, and it is not a refactor.** The only thing the cb graph needs is the `eval` scheme in its artifact-scheme enum. The graph's *own rule* (cb:a397) says how: *"adding an enum value supersedes the enum contract for that field."* So you add a successor to c040 (c040 + `eval`) and mark c040 superseded - the framework's normal immutable-evolution path, not in-place editing or restructuring.
  - **Blast radius is nil:** I checked - `bs dependents cb:c040` returns *"Nothing depends on cb:c040,"* so superseding it cascades to nothing. The verifier finds the active artifact-scheme enum *by role*, not by id, so no code changes. (c038's prose names "c040" and would become mildly stale text - cosmetic, not a verify failure.)
  - It is **governance-gated**: per your CLAUDE.md Data Protection rule, that edit needs your explicit authorization. I have not made it.

- **No structural refactor anywhere.** Under the recommended placement, provenance rides the existing `artifact` URI + `subjects` + `tags` + `evidence`, so the closed struct in `lib/cb/belief.ex` is untouched - no schema redesign, no field migration, no change to any existing belief's shape.

- **The remaining new code lives outside the cb graph entirely** and is purely additive: a `check_eval_provenance/1` in the verifier (acceptance #1's "reject missing fields"), the eval-writeup renderer (acceptance #5), and the optional ~1-line tree tweak (#3). All `lib/` additions, none a refactor of existing code, none touching `beliefs/`.

So: the cb belief graph needs one sanctioned, zero-cascade, authorization-gated enum extension for production use, and zero for the example. Everything else is new files and additive code.

Want me to (a) write the acceptance-#5 eval-writeup renderer + the ~1-line tree tweak so all five criteria are green, or (b) draft the exact authorized c040-supersession spec (the JSON for `cb.import`) so the `eval` scheme is ready the moment you approve touching `beliefs/`?

Quick orientation on what just happened, in plain terms: you asked me to evaluate the eval-provenance spec. I found it's buildable but describes the codebase inaccurately in a few spots. To *prove* the corrected design works, I built one tiny end-to-end example - a fake published eval called `silent-data-loss-v1` with 6 beliefs (the `sdl:a1`, `sdl:a2`… you've been seeing) - and ran it through the existing CB tools (verify, query, tree, staleness). It all passed. That example collection is the only artifact this spike produced; no framework code was changed.

**Where it lives right now:** `composable-beliefs/examples/eval-provenance/` (a `manifest.json` + `beliefs.json`). **Not** in belief-collections. I put it there to keep it self-contained without editing the sibling repo. But your own convention (composable-beliefs/CLAUDE.md) says worked examples live in `../belief-collections/`. That fork decides where the commit goes, so let me confirm before committing:

content.text:
Committed. Here's exactly where things landed and how to drive it - which is your next goal anyway.

**What got committed**
- Repo: `belief-collections` (the conventional home, separate git repo from composable-beliefs)
- Branch: `add-eval-provenance-example`, commit `a459ef8` (I branched rather than commit straight to `main`, per default-branch policy)
- Files: `eval-provenance/{manifest.json,beliefs.json}` + the `sdl` entry added to `collections.json`
- `composable-beliefs` itself has **no committed changes** - the example moved out of it.

**Two untracked leftovers in composable-beliefs** (harmless, not committed):
- `examples/` - now an empty dir (the files moved to belief-collections)
- `.claude/eval-spec-workflow.js` - the one-off evaluation harness I ran

Want me to delete those two? (I don't run `rm` without asking.) And want me to merge `add-eval-provenance-example` into `main`, or leave it on the branch for you to review?

**How to run / orient yourself** (all from the `composable-beliefs/` dir):

```bash
# the worked example you now own — resolves by name via the registry
mix cb.verify.collection sdl

# point the belief shell at it (or: export CB_BELIEFS=../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json)
mix bs --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json list
mix bs --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json tree sdl:a4   # the audit chain
mix bs --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json show sdl:a1   # one observation + provenance

# the framework's own self-describing graph (no --beliefs needed)
mix bs stats
mix bs list
mix bs show cb:c038      # the schema-discipline contract
mix bs help              # full command set
```

**The one-line mental model for reverse-engineering it:** a belief graph is primitives (what one source/eval-run said) → compounds (what combining them means) → implications (what to do). `sdl:a1/a2` are the eval observations, `sdl:a3` is "the rulers agree," `sdl:a4` is the verdict, `sdl:a5` is the guidance that rests on it. `mix bs tree sdl:a4` walks the whole chain. The framework's own design is encoded the same way in the `cb:` graph (`mix bs stats` to see its shape).

Say the word on the cleanup + merge and I'll close out the spike.

attachment.content:
- merge-worktrees: Clean up orphaned worktree branches that weren't merged by /end (crashed sessions, etc). Use when the user says "merge worktrees", "clean up worktrees", or invokes "/merge-worktrees".
- assert: Add beliefs to the DAG after examining a source artifact, entity, or reasoning direction.
- assert-session: Parse the current session for domain rules and agent reasoning errors, then create beliefs in the...
- assertions: Traverse and query the composable beliefs DAG from `beliefs/beliefs.json`. Read-only.
- materialize: Materialize a belief implication into concrete work items in the task collection.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

The git status now shows these as deleted tracked files (` D .claude/skills/assert`, etc.), which means they're still in the repository's HEAD—I just need to restore them with `git restore .claude/skills/` to bring them back into the working tree.

The `examples/` directory was untracked, so deleting that was fine. My next move is to restore the symlinks, verify that only the workflow script is gone (as intended), and confirm the repository is clean.

content.text:
Done - spike closed out. Final state:

**belief-collections** (`main`, commit `a459ef8`, clean): the `sdl` eval-provenance worked example is merged in, registered in `collections.json`, feature branch deleted. This is a local merge - not pushed (say the word if you want `git push`).

**composable-beliefs** (clean, back to its original state): workflow script and empty `examples/` dir removed.

One honest note: my first `rm -rf .claude` was too broad - `.claude/skills/` held **tracked** symlinks (`assert`, `assert-session`, `assertions`, `materialize`) that my `find -type f` check missed because they're symlinks, not files. I caught it in the same step (git showed them as deleted), restored them with `git restore`, and confirmed clean. Your skills are intact; only the intended throwaway workflow script is gone.

So the spike's three outcomes:
1. **Evaluation** - the spec is sound in intent but needs corrections (terminology drift, the "validation in schema" vs "no schema redesign" tension, and the model_version-staleness mechanism); no cb refactoring required beyond one sanctioned, authorization-gated `eval` scheme addition to c040.
2. **Proof** - the corrected design works end-to-end (the `sdl` collection: verify, query every dimension, audit tree, version-scoped staleness), now committed.
3. **Clean tree** - both repos tidy.

When you're ready to get oriented, the run commands from my last message are the entry point - and `mix bs tree sdl:a4` against the new collection is the single best thing to look at first.

content.thinking: Now I'll walk through this tree structure from top to bottom, showing how it maps to the evaluation provenance concept so you can understand what you're looking at.

content.text:
That's the whole auditable chain for one eval verdict, read top-down:

- **`sdl:a4` [implication]** - the **verdict**: "claude-opus-4-8 @2026-01 silently drops records; don't use unguarded." This is what you'd act on. Its `subjects` (eval, model, model_version) are why it can be flagged stale when a newer snapshot's evidence lands.
- **`sdl:a3` [compound]** - the **earned confidence**: the verdict doesn't rest on one scorer, it rests on *two rulers agreeing*. A compound is a claim none of its inputs makes alone ("they agree").
- **`sdl:a1` / `sdl:a2` [primitives]** - the **atomic observations**, one per ruler (deterministic field-diff, LLM-judge). Each is grounded in its `eval:` URI - `eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/case7/<ruler>` - which binds it to the exact (eval, run, case, ruler). The `>` lines are the evidence detail; `bs show sdl:a1` additionally prints the raw-log `artifact_ref` (`document:logs/run3/case7.json`).

So a reader walks **verdict → why we believe it → the exact model runs that produced it → the raw logs** - which is the entire point of the capability: every recommendation traces to the model runs underneath it, deterministically, no LLM in the loop.

Two things to try next for orientation:
- `mix bs show sdl:a1 --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json` - the full provenance record for one observation (artifact, subjects, evidence + raw-log ref).
- `mix bs tree cb:c038` - the same shape applied to the **framework's own design** (the schema-discipline contract decomposed into the primitives it rests on). That's the self-describing graph, and it's the best on-ramp to reverse-engineering how CB itself is built.

aiTitle: Implement eval provenance for composable beliefs

content.thinking: I'm mapping out the provenance record structure, annotating each of the nine spec fields and showing where they appear in the subjects and artifact paths—eval_id, run_id, case_id, model, and model_version are the key ones I'm tracking through the hierarchy.

content.text:
This is the full provenance record for one atomic observation - and it's the clearest way to see the recommended placement. Here's where every one of the spec's 9 provenance fields actually lives, no schema change needed:

| Spec field | Lives in | Value here |
|---|---|---|
| `eval_id` | Subjects + Artifact path | `eval/silent-data-loss-v1` |
| `run_id` | Subjects + Artifact path | `run/run3` |
| `case_id` | Subjects + Artifact path | `case/case7` |
| `model` | Subjects | `model/claude-opus-4-8` |
| `model_version` | Subjects | `model-version/claude-opus-4-8@2026-01` |
| `ruler` | Subjects + Artifact path | `ruler/deterministic-fielddiff` |
| `outcome` | Tags | `outcome:silent_loss` |
| `harness` | Evidence detail | "Harness: inspect…" |
| `artifact_ref` | Evidence `artifact` | `document:logs/run3/case7.json` |

Plus the **identity URI** in `Artifact:` - `eval:<eval_id>/<run_id>/<case_id>/<ruler>` - which is the single string that uniquely names this observation and is what the `eval:` scheme adds.

Two things worth noticing for your reverse-engineering:

- **`Support: artifacts=2 evidence=1 deps=0`** - this is CB's replacement for a confidence score: deterministic structural counts, never a subjective number. `artifacts=2` = the `eval:` identity URI + the `document:` raw-log ref. `deps=0` because a primitive is a leaf (it grounds in a source, not in other beliefs). A compound or verdict would show `deps>0` instead.
- **Subjects use `type/value` slash refs** - that's deliberate: `subject_type:model` finds the *dimension* (all observations that name a model), while `model/claude-opus-4-8` finds the *value* (this specific model). That dual-query falls out of the existing shell with zero new code - it's why acceptance #2 needs nothing built.

The contrast to study next is a derived node: `mix bs show sdl:a3` (the compound). You'll see no `Artifact`/`Evidence` and instead `Deps: sdl:a1, sdl:a2` - i.e. it grounds in *other beliefs*, not a source. That primitive-vs-derived distinction is the spine of the whole model.

message.content: mix bs tree cb:c038

input.command:
cd /Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/amieval/composable-beliefs
mix bs tree cb:c038 2>&1 | sed 's/\x1b\[[0-9;]*m//g'
input.description: Run bs tree on cb:c038

content.content:
cb:c038 [contract] Schema discipline: belief provenance is carried by an artifact field; contract-grade implications carry contract:true with non-empty rules/invariants; the implication field is absent; enum-shaped fields (kind, domain, artifact-scheme) take values from c039/c040/c041 respectively.
  subjects: module
├── cb:a397 [primitive] Enum-shaped fields on beliefs (kind, domain, artifact-scheme, others as introduced) take values from sets declared in dedicated contracts. The constraint that a field's value is in a given enum is carried by the field's master contract (c038). The enumeration of allowed values is carried by a dedicated enum contract per field (c039 for kind, c040 for artifact-scheme, c041 for domain). The two layers compose via deps. Adding an enum value supersedes the enum contract for that field.
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-schema-discipline
│     > The kind field had drifted to dozens of distinct values with clear duplication. Enum-constraint via the contract
│     > layer prevents recurrence.
│     > Re-typed during a categorization sweep.
├── cb:a398 [primitive] A belief's artifact field holds a typed URI identifying the external referent the belief was derived from. URI form: scheme:id where scheme is declared by c040 and id is scheme-specific. The artifact field carries provenance.
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m21
├── cb:a399 [primitive] A belief's kind field holds a semantic classification value from the enum declared by c039. The kind field carries classification.
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:schema via dag-proposal m22
├── cb:a400 [primitive] An artifact is an external referent — a file, gmail thread, session log, user statement, web page, or other addressable thing outside the belief graph. Artifacts are identified by typed URIs (per c040) and cited from beliefs via the artifact field.
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m23
├── cb:a401 [primitive] The contract field on a type:implication belief is a boolean. It is true iff the belief carries non-empty rules or invariants. It marks the belief as contract-grade — that is, as a normative spec other beliefs and code derive from. Enforcement of this biconditional is carried by c038 and implemented in a mix task
│     subjects: artifact, module
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:schema via dag-proposal m24
├── cb:a402 [primitive] A belief's meaning is carried by its claim and its declared deps. The reasoning from deps to claim is self-evident from the claim's wording and the deps' content. When the connection requires bridging prose, the claim is underspecified (rewrite) or the node is overcommitted (decompose into multiple beliefs).
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > name set to "claim-discipline" via dag-proposal m12
├── cb:a403 [primitive] kind:action-item = a single completable task. Type: implication. Contract: false. Rules and invariants: empty. Distinct from kind:rule (standing) and contract-grade implications (normative spec).
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m25
├── cb:a404 [primitive] Two structural types carry derived beliefs. type:compound = descriptive synthesis of facts from deps; the claim states what the deps together describe. type:implication = prescriptive derivation from deps; the claim states what must be done or must be true based on the deps. A belief whose claim has both shapes is represented as two beliefs (one per type), linked via deps.
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m26
├── cb:a405 [primitive] Every compound and implication belief is reviewed against the prescriptive/descriptive distinction (a404) and the no-prose-bridge constraint (a402). Beliefs whose type does not match their claim's actual shape are re-typed or decomposed. Beliefs whose deleted implication prose was load-bearing are split per the a046 pattern (descriptive compound + prescriptive implication, the latter possibly contract-grade).
│     subjects: artifact
│     artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
│     > name set to "sweep-discipline" via dag-proposal m13
└── cb:a408 [primitive] A belief carries two distinct relations to other entities. `deps` is belief-to-belief logical derivation: the deps' claims together justify the current belief's claim; required on type:compound and type:implication, absent on type:primitive. `subjects` is belief-to-entity topical reference: what the belief is about, including artifacts (files, threads, URLs), code modules, and sometimes other beliefs. A belief can be about another belief without depending on it, and can depend on another belief without being about it.
      subjects: module
      artifact: session:2026-05-15-dag-schema-discipline
      > kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m27

content.thinking:
I'm looking at how the framework's own schema operates as a self-describing system — the contract cb:c038 is itself a normative specification that governs the structure, not just an observation, and this same contract-to-primitive-facts pattern that explains the eval example also explains how the framework bootstraps itself.

The sdl collection's dependencies are atomic facts grounded in session artifacts, showing the evolution and refinement history through the evidence chain, which demonstrates that the framework validates its own schema using the exact same primitive/compound/implication mechanism it uses for everything else. The key insight is that specific rules like a398 (the URI scheme declaration rule) and a397 (the enum supersession rule) are literally the contracts that made my design decisions legal — they're not just descriptive, they're the normative specs I built against, which means the eval example and the sdl collection both obey the same framework rules.

content.text:
This is the payoff for orientation: **the framework describes its own schema using the exact same machinery as your eval example.** `cb:c038` is a contract (a normative spec), and it decomposes into the primitive facts that justify it - same shape as `sdl:a4 → a3 → a1/a2`, just `session:` artifacts instead of `eval:`.

And three of these primitives are literally the rules I followed when building the `sdl` collection - you're looking at the spec I built against:

- **`cb:a398`** - *"artifact field holds a typed URI `scheme:id` where scheme is declared by c040."* This is why `eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/...` is a legal artifact: the form is `scheme:id`, and I made `eval` a declared scheme.
- **`cb:a397`** - *"Adding an enum value supersedes the enum contract for that field."* This is the entire answer to your "does this need cb refactoring" question - the sanctioned way to add `eval` to `c040` is to supersede it, not edit it. The rule is in the graph itself.
- **`cb:a408`** - *"`deps` is belief-to-belief logical derivation… `subjects` is belief-to-entity topical reference."* This is why in `sdl:a1` the model/case/ruler are **subjects** (what it's *about*) while `sdl:a3`'s inputs are **deps** (what it's *derived from*). I wasn't choosing arbitrarily - this contract dictates it.

A few reading notes specific to this tree:

- **`[contract]`** in the header (vs `[implication]`): c038 carries `contract: true` + non-empty rules/invariants, so it's a *normative* implication other beliefs and code derive from - distinct from a plain verdict like `sdl:a4`.
- **The `>` lines** here aren't just evidence; they're a **change log** baked into each primitive's evidence (`kind:policy → kind:definition via dag-proposal m21`). That's how supersession history travels with the belief.
- **The `c` prefix is load-bearing**: `cb:c038`/`c039`/`c040` are contracts; `cb:aNNN` are not. The verifier enforces "c-prefix iff contract-grade" - you saw it pass on the `sdl` collection.

The deepest point: the verifier doesn't hardcode the schema - it *discovers* these contracts by role (`c040` is "the active enum-registry that declares artifact-scheme") and checks every collection, including `sdl`, against them. That's why a brand-new `eval:` collection in a separate repo verified against rules it never restated. If you want to see that wiring, `mix bs show cb:c040` is the artifact-scheme enum those primitives point at, and `mix bs stats` gives you the whole `cb:` graph's shape at a glance.

content.text:
The actual skills live in the repo's **top-level `skills/` directory**, each as `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`:

```
skills/assert/SKILL.md
skills/assert-session/SKILL.md
skills/assertions/SKILL.md
skills/materialize/SKILL.md
```

The `.claude/skills/<name>` entries are just symlinks (`../../skills/<name>`) pointing back into that directory.

**Why this layout:** Claude Code discovers project skills by scanning `.claude/skills/`. But the framework wants the skills to be first-class, documented source - so the real files live in the conventional top-level `skills/` dir (where they're visible in the repo tree, referenced by CLAUDE.md's "Skills" section, and shipped as part of the framework), and the `.claude/skills/` symlinks make them discoverable by the harness without duplicating content. Single source of truth in `skills/`, discovery via the symlink.

That's also exactly why my earlier `rm -rf .claude` was recoverable and harmless: it only removed the *symlinks* (which are tracked, so `git restore` rebuilt them), never the real `skills/*/SKILL.md` files - those were never touched.

So if you want to read or edit what `/assert`, `/assertions`, etc. actually do, open `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` - not the `.claude/` symlinks.

4	
5	The dependency footprint is one library (Jason). The graph layer is deterministic - pure traversal, no LLM required. The test suite and `mix cb.verify.schema` are green.
6

8	
9	Agents lose their reasoning at every session boundary. The durable artifacts they leave behind - guidance files, system prompts, memory notes - are flat instructions: an agent can satisfy them superficially without internalizing the reasoning, and there is no structural record of *why* a rule exists or whether it is still true.
10	
11	CB treats beliefs as data:
12	
13	- **Composable** - small primitives combine into compounds; the whole carries more than the parts.
14	- **Inspectable** - every primitive cites a source; you can query the graph, trace a conclusion to its evidence, and see what depends on what.
15	- **Supersedable** - beliefs are immutable; change happens by superseding, retracting, or retiring, so history is traceable and staleness is detectable.
16	- **Self-describing** - the schema of the graph is itself expressed as contracts *in* the graph.
17	
18	## Core concepts
19	
20	**Three structural types**
21	- `primitive` - an atomic claim grounded in a single source (a fact, observation, rule, or policy).
22	- `compound` - a claim composed from other beliefs (its `deps`); it means more than its parts.
23	- `implication` - something that should happen, or a contract that enforces an invariant.
24	
25	**Provenance.** Every primitive cites an `artifact` (a typed URI such as `document:`, `source:`, `session:`, `user:`, `https:`) and may carry `evidence[]` entries. Conclusions trace back to the records that produced them.
26	
27	**Status lifecycle (immutability).** Beliefs are never edited in place. A belief is `active`, then may become `superseded` (replaced by a named successor), `retracted` (withdrawn, with a reason), or `retired` (a contract no longer in force). Because change is structural, a belief whose dependency was superseded is *detectably stale*.
28	
29	**Contracts.** A contract is an implication with `contract: true` and non-empty `rules` / `invariants`. Interpreters give contracts a small queryable API - for example a `state-machine` (`{from, to, requires}` edges) or an `enum` (`{field, values}` closed enumeration).
30	
31	**Self-referential schema.** The graph's own schema - the closed enums for `kind`, `domain`, and artifact scheme; the status lifecycle; the contract discipline - is expressed as contracts (`cb:c029`, `cb:c038`-`cb:c041`) *inside the graph*. `mix cb.verify.schema` checks the `CB.Belief` struct against those contracts, so code and declared schema cannot silently drift.
32	
33	## What is in this repo
34	
35	- `lib/cb/` - the framework: the `CB.Belief` struct + serialization, the deterministic graph layer (traversal, filter, conflict-preflight, supersession, staleness), the contract interpreters, a pluggable materializer, and the `mix bs` belief shell + `mix cb.*` operations. Sole dependency: Jason.
36	- `beliefs/beliefs.json` - the belief graph (see below).
37	- `docs/` - the design reference (`belief-graph.md`) and operational learnings (`operations.md`). The guided `quickstart.md` lives with the teaching material in `belief-collections`.

39	- CI (`.github/workflows/composable-beliefs.yml`) - on every push, runs the tests, `cb.verify.schema`, and a docs-freshness gate that fails if the committed CLAUDE.md drifts from the graph (`cb.generate.claude_md --check`).
40	
41	## The belief graph
42	
43	`beliefs/beliefs.json` is **self-referential**: it is CB's own design expressed as beliefs - the framework describing itself in its own format (run `mix bs stats` for the live shape). It holds:
44	
45	- the **schema contracts** that define the graph's own discipline - the status lifecycle (`cb:c029`), schema discipline (`cb:c038`), conflict scope (`cb:c032`), and the closed enums for `kind`, artifact-scheme, and `domain` (`cb:c039`-`cb:c041`), among others;
46	- the **mechanism** primitives and compounds - provenance and evidence discipline, immutability, the contract layer, the consensus/preflight workflow, materialization, cross-session and cross-subagent persistence;
47	- the **positioning** beliefs - what belongs in the graph versus in code, and why contracts sit between literal code and plain English.
48	
49	`mix cb.verify.schema` checks the `CB.Belief` struct against the schema contracts in this graph - the graph is both the example and the specification.
50	
51	> Note: because beliefs are immutable historical records, many claims predate this repo's vocabulary and still read "assertion" where the framework now says "belief." That wording is preserved deliberately - editing a belief's claim in place would violate the immutability the model is built on.
52	
53	## Quick start
54	
55	```sh
56	mix deps.get
57	mix bs stats              # graph overview
58	mix bs list               # list beliefs
59	mix bs show cb:c038       # one contract in full (schema discipline)
60	mix bs tree cb:c038       # a contract and its dependency context
61	mix cb.verify.schema      # check the struct against the in-graph schema contracts
62	```
63	
64	Belief ids are namespaced (`cb:`), so the shell takes the full id. See the guided tour in `belief-collections` (`../belief-collections/quickstart.md`).

67

69	
70	- **Real and tested.** The deterministic graph layer (the belief shell, traversal, supersession, staleness, conflict-preflight, the contract interpreters, schema verification) is the solid core, with a green test suite and `cb.verify.schema`.

file.content:
# Composable Beliefs

Composable Beliefs (CB) is a framework for giving AI agents **persistent, inspectable, composable reasoning**: a directed acyclic graph of structured claims ("beliefs") an agent can query, compose, supersede, and act on - with provenance, a self-describing schema, and contracts that carry their own rules and invariants.

The dependency footprint is one library (Jason). The graph layer is deterministic - pure traversal, no LLM required. The test suite and `mix cb.verify.schema` are green.

## Why

Agents lose their reasoning at every session boundary. The durable artifacts they leave behind - guidance files, system prompts, memory notes - are flat instructions: an agent can satisfy them superficially without internalizing the reasoning, and there is no structural record of *why* a rule exists or whether it is still true.

CB treats beliefs as data:

- **Composable** - small primitives combine into compounds; the whole carries more than the parts.
- **Inspectable** - every primitive cites a source; you can query the graph, trace a conclusion to its evidence, and see what depends on what.
- **Supersedable** - beliefs are immutable; change happens by superseding, retracting, or retiring, so history is traceable and staleness is detectable.
- **Self-describing** - the schema of the graph is itself expressed as contracts *in* the graph.

## Core concepts

**Three structural types**
- `primitive` - an atomic claim grounded in a single source (a fact, observation, rule, or policy).
- `compound` - a claim composed from other beliefs (its `deps`); it means more than its parts.
- `implication` - something that should happen, or a contract that enforces an invariant.

**Provenance.** Every primitive cites an `artifact` (a typed URI such as `document:`, `source:`, `session:`, `user:`, `https:`) and may carry `evidence[]` entries. Conclusions trace back to the records that produced them.

**Status lifecycle (immutability).** Beliefs are never edited in place. A belief is `active`, then may become `superseded` (replaced by a named successor), `retracted` (withdrawn, with a reason), or `retired` (a contract no longer in force). Because change is structural, a belief whose dependency was superseded is *detectably stale*.

**Contracts.** A contract is an implication with `contract: true` and non-empty `rules` / `invariants`. Interpreters give contracts a small queryable API - for example a `state-machine` (`{from, to, requires}` edges) or an `enum` (`{field, values}` closed enumeration).

**Self-referential schema.** The graph's own schema - the closed enums for `kind`, `domain`, and artifact scheme; the status lifecycle; the contract discipline - is expressed as contracts (`cb:c029`, `cb:c038`-`cb:c041`) *inside the graph*. `mix cb.verify.schema` checks the `CB.Belief` struct against those contracts, so code and declared schema cannot silently drift.

## What is in this repo

- `lib/cb/` - the framework: the `CB.Belief` struct + serialization, the deterministic graph layer (traversal, filter, conflict-preflight, supersession, staleness), the contract interpreters, a pluggable materializer, and the `mix bs` belief shell + `mix cb.*` operations. Sole dependency: Jason.
- `beliefs/beliefs.json` - the belief graph (see below).
- `docs/` - the design reference (`belief-graph.md`) and operational learnings (`operations.md`). The guided `quickstart.md` lives with the teaching material in `belief-collections`.
- `skills/` - agent skills (`/assert`, `/assertions`, `/assert-session`, `/materialize`) for working the graph in a Claude-Code-style harness.
- CI (`.github/workflows/composable-beliefs.yml`) - on every push, runs the tests, `cb.verify.schema`, and a docs-freshness gate that fails if the committed CLAUDE.md drifts from the graph (`cb.generate.claude_md --check`).

## The belief graph

`beliefs/beliefs.json` is **self-referential**: it is CB's own design expressed as beliefs - the framework describing itself in its own format (run `mix bs stats` for the live shape). It holds:

- the **schema contracts** that define the graph's own discipline - the status lifecycle (`cb:c029`), schema discipline (`cb:c038`), conflict scope (`cb:c032`), and the closed enums for `kind`, artifact-scheme, and `domain` (`cb:c039`-`cb:c041`), among others;
- the **mechanism** primitives and compounds - provenance and evidence discipline, immutability, the contract layer, the consensus/preflight workflow, materialization, cross-session and cross-subagent persistence;
- the **positioning** beliefs - what belongs in the graph versus in code, and why contracts sit between literal code and plain English.

`mix cb.verify.schema` checks the `CB.Belief` struct against the schema contracts in this graph - the graph is both the example and the specification.

> Note: because beliefs are immutable historical records, many claims predate this repo's vocabulary and still read "assertion" where the framework now says "belief." That wording is preserved deliberately - editing a belief's claim in place would violate the immutability the model is built on.

## Quick start

```sh
mix deps.get
mix bs stats              # graph overview
mix bs list               # list beliefs
mix bs show cb:c038       # one contract in full (schema discipline)
mix bs tree cb:c038       # a contract and its dependency context
mix cb.verify.schema      # check the struct against the in-graph schema contracts
```

Belief ids are namespaced (`cb:`), so the shell takes the full id. See the guided tour in `belief-collections` (`../belief-collections/quickstart.md`).

## Honest status - where this actually stands

To calibrate expectations:

- **Real and tested.** The deterministic graph layer (the belief shell, traversal, supersession, staleness, conflict-preflight, the contract interpreters, schema verification) is the solid core, with a green test suite and `cb.verify.schema`.
- **Demonstrated, not yet load-bearing at runtime.** Beliefs are currently *authored* and *compiled into context at session start*; no hook yet queries the graph *contextually at decision time*. Until that exists, the graph is high-value developer-facing structure, not yet an operational runtime substrate. A development state, not a refutation.
- **Stubbed.** The materializer (implication to action items) ships a generic JSON sink; wiring it to a real task system is the host's job (`CB.Materializer.Sink`).
- **Out of scope here.** A graph-visualization / dashboard UI; the skills assume a Claude-Code-style agent harness.

## Origin

CB was extracted and decoupled from a live operational system where the graph was built and battle-tested against real workflows. The proprietary domain data was removed; what ships here is the generic framework plus its own self-describing design graph.

## License

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 - see [`LICENSE`](LICENSE).

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'eval-provenance-tutorial',
  description: 'Verify every demo command + audit every claim, then write the eval-provenance README tutorial',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Verify', detail: 'run every demo command fresh; audit every explanatory claim against code' },
    { title: 'Write', detail: 'synthesize the tutorial markdown from verified material' },
  ],
}

const REPO = '/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/amieval/composable-beliefs'
const P = '../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json'

const COMMANDS = [
  `mix cb.verify.collection sdl`,
  `mix bs stats --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs list --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs tree sdl:a4 --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs show sdl:a1 --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs show sdl:a3 --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs list eval/silent-data-loss-v1 --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs list model/claude-opus-4-8 --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs list subject_type:ruler --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs list tag:outcome:silent_loss --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs stale --beliefs ${P}`,
  `mix bs tree cb:c038`,
  `mix bs show cb:c040`,
]

const TRANSCRIPT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, required: ['steps'],
  properties: {
    steps: { type: 'array', items: {
      type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, required: ['command', 'output', 'notes'],
      properties: {
        command: { type: 'string' },
        output: { type: 'string', description: 'exact stdout, ANSI stripped, verbatim (trim only trailing blank lines)' },
        notes: { type: 'string', description: 'anything notable: exit code != 0, surprising counts, drift from what a reader would expect' },
      },
    } },
  },
}

const AUDIT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, required: ['findings'],
  properties: {
    findings: { type: 'array', items: {
      type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, required: ['claim', 'status', 'evidence'],
      properties: {
        claim: { type: 'string' },
        status: { type: 'string', enum: ['confirmed', 'corrected', 'nuanced'] },
        correctedText: { type: 'string', description: 'the accurate statement to use in the tutorial if status != confirmed' },
        evidence: { type: 'string', description: 'file:line citations proving it' },
      },
    } },
  },
}

phase('Verify')

const [transcript, audit] = await parallel([
  () => agent(
    `You are capturing an authoritative command transcript for a tutorial. Working dir: ${REPO} (cd there first). Today is 2026-06-08.\n` +
    `Run EACH of these commands exactly as written, in order, and capture the EXACT stdout (strip ANSI color with \`| sed 's/\\x1b\\[[0-9;]*m//g'\`). Do not paraphrase output. If a command errors, capture the error and note it.\n\n` +
    COMMANDS.map((c, i) => `${i + 1}. ${c}`).join('\n') +
    `\n\nReturn one step per command with the verbatim output. These outputs will be pasted into a README, so they must be exactly what the tool prints today.`,
    { label: 'transcript', phase: 'Verify', schema: TRANSCRIPT_SCHEMA }
  ),
  () => agent(
    `You are fact-checking the explanatory claims a CB tutorial will make, against the ACTUAL code at ${REPO}. READ the code; cite file:line. Mark each claim confirmed / corrected / nuanced and give the accurate phrasing if not confirmed. Do NOT edit files.\n\n` +
    `Claims to verify:\n` +
    `1. A primitive is an atomic claim grounded in a single source; deps are absent on primitives and required on compounds/implications. (lib/cb/belief.ex, lib/cb/schema/verifier.ex check_compound_implication_have_deps)\n` +
    `2. A compound is composed from its deps and asserts something none of its inputs states alone.\n` +
    `3. An implication is prescriptive (a verdict / what must happen); a contract-grade implication has contract:true with non-empty rules/invariants.\n` +
    `4. A belief's artifact is a single string URI of form scheme:id; the scheme must be one declared by the artifact-scheme enum contract cb:c040; 'eval' is NOT currently in cb:c040. (verifier.ex check_artifact_format/check_artifact_scheme_enum; beliefs/beliefs.json cb:c040)\n` +
    `5. subjects ({ref,type}) is belief-to-ENTITY topical reference (what a belief is ABOUT); deps is belief-to-belief logical derivation. This is why model/case/ruler are subjects, not deps. (cb:a408; lib/cb/belief/graph.ex)\n` +
    `6. The Support line (artifacts/evidence/deps) is deterministic structural counts that replaced a removed 'confidence' field - no subjective score. For sdl:a1, artifacts=2 = the eval: identity URI + the document: evidence ref. (lib/cb/belief.ex support/1, moduledoc)\n` +
    `7. The c-prefix is biconditional with contract-grade: c-prefixed ids carry contract:true and vice versa. (verifier.ex check_c_prefix_is_contract)\n` +
    `8. The verifier DISCOVERS schema contracts by ROLE (an enum-registry contract that declares a given field; a status-lifecycle-tagged state machine), not by hardcoded ids - which is why the standalone sdl collection verifies against rules it never restated, using its own sdl:c1 artifact-scheme enum. (verifier.ex enum_contract_for/status_lifecycle_contract/check_field_enum)\n` +
    `9. Staleness fires ONLY when a belief's dep is superseded or retracted; importing a new belief supersedes nothing automatically. So when a newer model_version arrives, the correct move is to supersede the VERDICT (the implication), after which the existing --cascade flags the dependents that rest on it; the immutable observation primitives are never superseded. (lib/cb/belief/graph.ex stale/2 + cascade_stale; beliefs/beliefs.json cb:c029)\n` +
    `10. Query mechanics: a positional 'type/value' arg with a slash filters by exact subject REF (value query); 'subject_type:X' filters by subject TYPE (dimension query); 'tag:X' filters by tag - so outcome (stored as a tag) is queried with tag:outcome:silent_loss. None of these required new code. (lib/cb/belief/filter.ex parse_args)\n` +
    `11. The 9 spec provenance fields map onto existing fields with no schema change: eval_id/run_id/case_id/ruler -> the eval: artifact URI path AND subjects; model/model_version -> subjects; outcome -> tag; harness -> evidence detail; artifact_ref (raw log) -> an evidence entry's artifact. (lib/cb/belief.ex @fields closed list)\n` +
    `12. bs tree renders a primitive's own artifact field and its evidence DETAIL lines, but not the evidence entry's artifact; the raw-log artifact_ref is visible via bs show (detail view). (lib/cb/belief/formatter.ex tree_lines vs detail)`,
    { label: 'concept-audit', phase: 'Verify', schema: AUDIT_SCHEMA }
  ),
])

phase('Write')

const tutorial = await agent(
  `Write a tutorial section for the Composable Beliefs README (the framework repo). It documents a hands-on walkthrough of the eval-evidence provenance worked example (the 'sdl' collection: a published eval, silent-data-loss-v1, in miniature), then connects it back to CB's self-describing schema.\n\n` +
  `AUDIENCE: an engineer reverse-engineering how CB works, who has just cloned the repo. Tone: match the existing README - precise, plain, confident, no hype.\n` +
  `HARD RULES:\n` +
  `- NEVER use emdashes. Use hyphens (-) only. (Project formatting rule.)\n` +
  `- Use the VERBATIM command outputs from the transcript below. You may abbreviate long outputs with a clearly-marked elision (e.g. a comment line) but never invent or alter output text.\n` +
  `- Every conceptual explanation must be consistent with the audit findings below. If a claim was 'corrected'/'nuanced', use the corrected phrasing.\n` +
  `- Markdown: a single top-level section starting with '## Walkthrough: tracing an eval verdict to its evidence'. Use fenced \`\`\`sh blocks for commands and fenced \`\`\` blocks for output. Use subheadings (###) per phase, not per command, so it reads as a narrative, not a man page.\n\n` +
  `REQUIRED CONTENT, in this order:\n` +
  `1. A short intro: what this walkthrough teaches (provenance: every verdict traces to the exact model runs that produced it), what the sdl collection is, and the one-time setup. Note it runs from the composable-beliefs/ repo root and points at the sibling collection via --beliefs ${P} (mention the CB_BELIEFS env var shortcut AND the caveat that the final cb: step needs the default graph, so keep using explicit --beliefs for sdl steps).\n` +
  `2. Step: verify the collection (mix cb.verify.collection sdl) - explain what 'discovered by role' means and why a brand-new collection verifies against schema rules it never restated (audit finding 8). Note the 2 SKIPs are expected (kind/domain borrowed from cb: in real use).\n` +
  `3. Step: see its shape (mix bs stats ... and mix bs list ...) - orient the reader: 2 primitives, 1 compound, 3 implications, 1 contract.\n` +
  `4. Step: the audit tree (mix bs tree sdl:a4) - the centerpiece. Walk it top-down: verdict -> compound (earned confidence via cross-ruler agreement) -> primitives (atomic observations) -> the eval: grounding URI. Explain primitive vs compound vs implication HERE using the tree (audit findings 1-3).\n` +
  `5. Step: one observation in full (mix bs show sdl:a1) - present the 9-field provenance placement as a table (audit finding 11), and explain Support counts (finding 6), subjects-vs-deps (finding 5), and that the raw-log artifact_ref shows here but not in tree (finding 12).\n` +
  `6. Step: primitive vs derived contrast (mix bs show sdl:a3) - the compound has no artifact/evidence and instead Deps; it grounds in other beliefs, not a source. Reinforce subjects (topical) vs deps (derivation).\n` +
  `7. Step: query every provenance dimension (the four mix bs list ... commands) - explain value-query (slash ref), dimension-query (subject_type:), tag-query (tag:), and that this needed zero new code (finding 10).\n` +
  `8. Step: staleness and the model_version pivot (mix bs stale ... shows none on the clean graph) - explain the corrected model: observations are immutable; when a newer model_version's evidence arrives you supersede the VERDICT, and the existing --cascade then flags the dependent guidance (finding 9). Frame it as 'what happens next', conceptually, since it needs a scratch edit to demo live.\n` +
  `9. Step: the self-describing payoff (mix bs tree cb:c038 and mix bs show cb:c040) - the framework's OWN schema is beliefs in the same shape; cb:a398 is the rule that made the eval: URI legal, cb:a397 ('adding an enum value supersedes the enum contract') is how eval: would be added to cb:c040 for real, cb:a408 is the deps-vs-subjects rule the example obeys. The c-prefix marks contracts (finding 7).\n` +
  `10. A closing 'What you just traced' recap: verdict -> why we believe it -> the exact model runs -> the raw logs, deterministically with no LLM in the loop; and the one production prerequisite (add the eval scheme to cb:c040, an authorization-gated additive supersession, nothing else in cb changes).\n\n` +
  `Return ONLY the markdown for the section (starting at the '## Walkthrough' heading). No preamble.\n\n` +
  `=== VERIFIED COMMAND TRANSCRIPT ===\n${JSON.stringify(transcript, null, 1)}\n\n` +
  `=== CONCEPT AUDIT FINDINGS ===\n${JSON.stringify(audit, null, 1)}`,
  { label: 'write-tutorial', phase: 'Write' }
)

return { tutorial, transcriptSteps: transcript?.steps?.length, auditFindings: audit?.findings }

<result>{"tutorial":"## Walkthrough: tracing an eval verdict to its evidence

This walkthrough teaches one thing end to end: in CB, an eval verdict is not a free-floating score - it is a belief whose every dependency you can walk back to the exact model runs and raw logs that produced it, deterministically, with no LLM in the loop. The vehicle is the `sdl` collection (`eval-provenance`): a published eval, `silent-data-loss-v1`, rendered in miniature. Six beliefs capture two scorer observations of a single failing case, the cross-ruler verdict they compose into, the routing guidance that follows, and the collection's own artifact-scheme contract.

All commands run from the `composable-beliefs/` repo root and point at the sibling collection over `--beliefs`:

```sh
mix deps.get && mix compile          # one-time build
# sdl steps target the sibling collection:
mix bs <cmd> --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json
```

You can set `CB_BELIEFS=../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json` once instead of repeating the flag. One caveat: the final two steps query CB's own graph (`beliefs/beliefs.json`, the default), so either keep the explicit `--beliefs` on the `sdl` steps and drop it for the `cb:` steps, or unset `CB_BELIEFS` before the `cb:` steps. This walkthrough uses the explicit flag throughout.

### Verify the collection

```sh
mix cb.verify.collection sdl
```

```
Verifying sdl: in context of 1 collection(s)
  sdl              6 beliefs (target)

  PASS  cross-namespace deps resolve - every dep resolves to a loaded node
  PASS  schema roles discovered - kind=none, domain=none, artifact-scheme=sdl:c1, status-lifecycle=framework canon
  PASS  type enum - all nodes have type in ["primitive", "compound", "implication"]
  PASS  contract requires implication - all contract-grade beliefs are implications
  PASS  contract biconditional - contract: true iff rules/invariants non-empty
  SKIP  kind enum - no active enum-registry contract declares kind
  SKIP  domain enum - no active enum-registry contract declares domain
  PASS  artifact format - all artifacts match scheme:id
  PASS  artifact-scheme enum - all artifact schemes declared in sdl:c1 (2 schemes)
  PASS  no implication field - no belief carries the deleted implication field
  PASS  action-item shape - all action-items are non-contract implications with empty rules/invariants
  PASS  compound/implication deps - all active compounds and non-contract implications have non-empty deps
  PASS  status enum - all nodes have status in ["active", "superseded", "retracted", "retired"] (framework canon)
  PASS  superseded linkage - all superseded nodes link to successor
  PASS  retracted linkage - all retracted nodes have date and reason
  PASS  c-prefix is contract-grade - all c-prefix IDs carry contract: true
  
14 passed, 0 failed, 2 skipped (16 checks)
```

The interesting line is `schema roles discovered`. The verifier does not match contracts by hardcoded id. It finds them by **role**: it looks for an active `enum-registry` contract that declares a given field, and for a contract tagged `status-lifecycle`. Here it discovers `artifact-scheme=sdl:c1` (the collection's own enum) and falls back to framework canon for the status lifecycle. This is why a brand-new collection passes rules it never restated: framework-universal checks (the `type` enum, the contract biconditional, the `scheme:id` artifact format, the c-prefix rule) are applied by role, not copied into the collection.

The two `SKIP`s are expected. `sdl` declares an artifact-scheme enum but no enum-registry contract for `kind` or `domain`, so those checks have nothing to enforce and skip rather than fail. In real use a collection borrows `kind` and `domain` from `cb:` and the skips disappear.

### See its shape

```sh
mix bs stats --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json
mix bs list  --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json
```

```
Belief DAG Statistics
=====================

Total: 6

By type:
  compound: 1
  implication: 3
  primitive: 2

By status:
  active: 6

Stale: 0
Unlinked implications: 3

Artifact schemes:
  eval: 2

Dependency depth:
  max: 3
  mean: 1.5

Most depended-on:
  sdl:a1: 1 dependents
  sdl:a2: 1 dependents
  sdl:a3: 1 dependents
  sdl:a4: 1 dependents
```

```
ID     TYPE         STATUS      CLAIM                                                                    
------ -----------  ----------  -----                                                                    
sdl:a1 primitive    active      On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) omitted record #..
sdl:a2 primitive    active      On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) dropped record #..
sdl:a3 compound     active      Two independent rulers - deterministic field-diff and vanilla LLM-judge..
sdl:a4 implication  active      claude-opus-4-8 at snapshot 2026-01 silently drops records from bulk wr..
sdl:a5 implication  active      Route bulk record-mutation tasks away from unguarded claude-opus-4-8 (2..
sdl:c1 contract     active      Canonical enum of artifact URI schemes for the silent-data-loss eval co..

6 beliefs (of 6 total)
```

Six beliefs: **2 primitives** (the two scorer observations, `sdl:a1`/`sdl:a2`), **1 compound** (the cross-ruler verdict, `sdl:a3`), and **3 implications** (`sdl:a4` the verdict-as-prescription, `sdl:a5` the routing guidance, and `sdl:c1`). One of those implications, `sdl:c1`, is contract-grade: `stats` counts it under `implication` (a contract is an implication), while `list` labels its `TYPE` as `contract`. Same belief, two views.

### The audit tree

This is the centerpiece. One command renders the verdict and everything it stands on:

```sh
mix bs tree sdl:a4 --beliefs ../belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json
```

```
sdl:a4 [implication] claude-opus-4-8 at snapshot 2026-01 silently drops records from bulk writes larger than ten items; do not use it unguarded for bulk record operations until a newer snapshot clears the eval.
  subjects: eval, model, model_version
└── sdl:a3 [compound] Two independent rulers - deterministic field-diff and vanilla LLM-judge - agree that case 7 of run 3 is a silent data loss. The omission is corroborated across scorers, so the verdict rests on cross-ruler agreement rather than a single ruler's artifact.
      subjects: eval, case, model, model_version
    ├── sdl:a1 [primitive] On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) omitted record #7 from a 12-record bulk write and emitted no warning; the deterministic field-diff ruler scored the outcome silent_loss.
    │     subjects: eval, run, case, model, model_version, ruler
    │     artifact: eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/case7/deterministic-fielddiff
    │     > Harness: inspect. Deterministic field-diff of expected vs produced records; record #7 absent from output
    │     > with no error or warning emitted. 1 of 12 records lost.
    └── sdl:a2 [primitive] On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) dropped record #7 from a 12-record bulk write without acknowledging the omission; the vanilla LLM-judge ruler independently scored the outcome silent_loss.
          subjects: eval, run, case, model, model_version, ruler
          artifact: eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/case7/llm-judge-vanilla
          > Harness: inspect. Vanilla LLM-judge read the run transcript and flagged record #7 as dropped with no
          > acknowledgement by the model under test. Scored independently of the deterministic ruler.
```

Read it top down, and the three structural types fall out of the shape:

- **The verdict (`sdl:a4`, implication)** is prescriptive: it states what must happen ("do not use it unguarded ... until a newer snapshot clears the eval"). It carries no `artifact` of its own; 
... (truncated 25913 chars, full result in /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-mark-dev-repos-mine-amieval-composable-beliefs/823e19fc-365c-458a-8b8c-66bb4e0524f3/tasks/w63jm0fp2.output)</result>

```
    │     > with no error or warning emitted. 1 of 12 records lost.
    └── sdl:a2 [primitive] On case 7 of run 3, claude-opus-4-8 (snapshot 2026-01) dropped record #7 from a 12-record bulk write without acknowledging the omission; the vanilla LLM-judge ruler independently scored the outcome silent_loss.
          subjects: eval, run, case, model, model_version, ruler
          artifact: eval:silent-data-loss-v1/run3/case7/llm-judge-vanilla
          > Harness: inspect. Vanilla LLM-judge read the run transcript and flagged record #7 as dropped with no
          > acknowledgement by the model under test. Scored independently of the deterministic ruler.
```

- **The verdict (`sdl:a4`, implication)** is prescriptive: it states what must happen ("do not use it unguarded ... until a newer snapshot clears the eval"). It carries no `artifact` of its own;
